title: Teaching
text: My teaching interests connect physiology, anatomy, disease ecology, wildlife biology, One Health, and reproducible research.

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching pathways


Biological foundations
Physiology, anatomy, comparative biology, and organismal function.


Applied context
Wildlife health, disease ecology, conservation, and One Health case studies.


Research skills
R, GitHub, Quarto, data organization, visualization, and reproducible workflows.


Mentorship
Student training, professional development, scientific writing, and confidence-building.

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching overview
I am interested in teaching biology in a way that connects concepts to real organisms, ecosystems, communities, and health problems. My teaching approach emphasizes clarity, application, visual learning, and the connection between biological mechanisms and real-world conservation or health challenges.

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching model


Concept
Start with core biological principles and clear explanations.


Context
Connect concepts to animals, humans, ecosystems, disease, and conservation.


Practice
Use examples, diagrams, data, and applied cases to strengthen understanding.


Translation
Help students communicate science clearly and confidently.

title: Teaching
text:
Mentorship
I value mentoring students through research experiences, field sampling, laboratory techniques, scientific writing, data analysis, GitHub workflows, and professional development.
An important part of my work is helping students see themselves as capable contributors to science, especially in Puerto Rican and island-based research contexts.

title: Publications & Presentations
text:
Manuscripts in preparation

First epidemiological assessment of Toxoplasma gondii serological testing in Puerto Rico
Working title: First epidemiological assessment of Toxoplasma gondii serological testing in Puerto Rico: an analysis of NBS human diagnostic records
This manuscript uses human diagnostic testing records from Puerto Rico to characterize Toxoplasma gondii exposure patterns across demographic, diagnostic, and regional variables.
Status: In preparation
Research areas: Epidemiology · One Health · Public health · Reproducible R analysis

title: Outreach
text: Community-based science, student training, conservation education, and public-facing One Health communication.

title: Outreach
text:
Outreach pathways


Community
Conservation education connected to local ecosystems, species, and environmental concerns.


Students
Research exposure, fieldwork, laboratory skills, data literacy, and professional development.


WildCiencia
Public-facing science communication, visual storytelling, and future educational resources.


Conservation action
Accessible science that supports belonging, care, and protection of local spaces.

title: Outreach
text:
Outreach vision
I believe conservation becomes stronger when communities are not treated only as audiences, but as partners, knowledge holders, and caretakers of local spaces.
My outreach work is guided by the idea that people protect places more deeply when they develop belonging in those places.

title: Outreach
text:
Community engagement model


Listen
Understand community connections to local ecosystems, species, and environmental concerns.


Translate
Turn technical research into accessible language, visuals, stories, and educational materials.


Train
Support students through research experiences, fieldwork, laboratory exposure, and communication skills.


Return
Bring research knowledge back to communities through outreach, education, and public-facing resources.

title: Outreach
text:
Featured outreach stories
These selected outreach stories document community-based conservation, student training, public education, media engagement, and science communication connected to my work in wildlife disease ecology, One Health, and conservation science.





Manatee Conservation Night outreach event




Manatee Conservation Night

Community conservation Manatee Project Puerto Rico One Health outreach

Focus: Manatee conservation, coastal ecosystems, environmental health, and community science
Format: Community event, educational activities, and public conservation discussion
Related project: Manatee Project Puerto Rico
Manatee Conservation Night was developed as a community-centered outreach activity connecting marine mammal conservation, environmental health, and public science communication. The event created a space to discuss the conservation of the Caribbean manatee, the importance of coastal ecosystems, and the ways communities can participate in conservation action.
This activity reflects my broader commitment to science that returns to communities, especially in Puerto Rican coastal and island contexts where people, wildlife, watersheds, and ecosystems are deeply connected.







Operation Viral Christmas outreach flyer




Operation Viral Christmas

Community outreach Student engagement Science and service

Focus: Community support, student-led engagement, and science-connected outreach
Format: Holiday outreach initiative
Related themes: Community-based science, student training, public engagement
Operation Viral Christmas is a community-oriented outreach initiative that connects science, service, and student engagement. The activity reflects the idea that scientific communities can contribute beyond the laboratory by supporting local communities and building a culture of care, belonging, and public connection.
This initiative also represents the type of outreach I want to continue developing through WildCiencia: science that is visible, human, accessible, and connected to the people and places it serves.







Teacher workshop field collection activity




Teacher and Student Conservation Workshops

Teacher workshop Hands-on science Environmental education

Focus: Field sampling, aquatic organisms, conservation education, and laboratory exposure
Format: Workshop and hands-on training
Related themes: Environmental education, aquatic systems, field-to-lab learning
These workshops connect classroom learning with field and laboratory experiences. Activities such as aquatic sampling, organism observation, processing, and microscopy help participants connect environmental systems with biological research and conservation questions.







CienciaPR outreach activity




CienciaPR School Outreach

K–12 outreach Science communication Puerto Rican science

Focus: Science education, conservation awareness, and student engagement
Format: School-based outreach
Related themes: Puerto Rican science, environmental education, youth engagement
CienciaPR outreach activities create opportunities to communicate science with younger audiences and connect biological concepts to Puerto Rican ecosystems, conservation, and environmental health.







Radio interview and public communication




Media and Public Communication

Media outreach Public communication Manatee Project Puerto Rico

Focus: Public communication of conservation science
Format: Media/radio engagement
Related themes: Manatee conservation, public science communication, outreach visibility
Media engagement helps translate research beyond academic spaces and reach broader audiences. This type of communication is central to building visibility for conservation work, community science, and future WildCiencia resources.

title: Alan J. Figueroa Ruiz
text:
Welcome
I am a conservation agronomist and master’s student in Biology at the University of Puerto Rico at Mayagüez. My academic background combines Animal Science, Natural Resources Conservation and Development, and Environmental Sociology and Public Policy.
My research sits at the intersection of wildlife disease ecology, conservation physiology, One Health, environmental health, reproductive biology, and reproducible research. I am interested in how pathogen exposure, landscape change, stormwater runoff, urbanization, and human activity affect threatened wildlife populations and the ecosystems they depend on.
This website serves as my main academic portal. It connects my research, teaching, outreach, publications, CV, and future project-specific websites.

title: Alan J. Figueroa Ruiz
section: From island landscapes to wildlife health
text:
From island landscapes to wildlife health
My academic path connects agriculture, animal science, natural resources, environmental sociology, and biology. I am especially interested in Puerto Rican island systems because they make ecological connections visible: mountains to rivers, cities to estuaries, cats to coastal waters, pathogens to wildlife, and communities to conservation action.
This portal brings together my research, project websites, outreach, teaching, CV, and future platforms.

title: Alan J. Figueroa Ruiz
text:
Conservation question
How do pathogens move through human-modified landscapes, and what does that movement mean for wildlife, ecosystems, and communities?
My work approaches this question from Puerto Rico’s land-sea interface, where watersheds, coastal ecosystems, community animals, humans, and endangered marine mammals are connected through shared environmental pathways.

title: Alan J. Figueroa Ruiz
text:
Field-to-conservation framework


1. Landscapes
Watersheds, coastal cities, estuaries, mangroves, seagrass habitats, and manatee-use areas.


2. Exposure
Environmental contamination, runoff, stormwater pathways, and potential pathogen transport.


3. Evidence
Field metadata, environmental samples, microscopy, molecular diagnostics, and reproducible analysis.


4. Action
Conservation interpretation, student training, outreach, and community-centered communication.

title: About
text: I am a conservation agronomist and master’s student in Biology at the University of Puerto Rico at Mayagüez, working across wildlife disease ecology, conservation physiology, One Health, reproductive biology, and environmental health.

title: About
text:
Academic background
My academic path combines Animal Science, Natural Resources Management, Conservation and Development, and Environmental Sociology and Public Policy. This combination shaped how I think about biology: not only as the study of organisms, but also as the study of ecosystems, landscapes, communities, policy, and the social conditions that influence conservation outcomes.
My current graduate work focuses on Toxoplasma gondii at the land-sea interface in Puerto Rico, with emphasis on environmental transmission pathways and conservation implications for the endangered Caribbean manatee.

title: About
text:
Find my path


Academic formation
Animal Science, Natural Resources Conservation Management and Development, Environmental Sociology, and Public Policy.


Research direction
Wildlife medicine and disease ecology, One Health, conservation physiology, reproductive biology, environmental diagnostics, and reproducible science.


Conservation identity
Puerto Rican island science, watershed-to-coast thinking, landscape thinking, wildlife health, and community-centered conservation.

title: About
text:
Academic path



1


Agricultural and animal science foundation
My academic formation began in Animal Science, where I developed a technical foundation in agriculture, animal biology, physiology, production systems, and applied biological sciences.

Animal Science Agriculture Applied biology





2


Conservation, natural resources, and society
I expanded my training through Natural Resources Conservation and Development, Environmental Sociology, and Public Policy. This helped me think about conservation as both a biological and social process shaped by ecosystems, communities, policy, and environmental change.

Natural resources Environmental sociology Public policy





3


Transition into wildlife health and disease ecology
My interests moved toward wildlife disease ecology, conservation physiology, One Health, and the ways pathogens, landscapes, domestic animals, humans, and wildlife are connected through shared environments.

Wildlife disease ecology Conservation physiology One Health





4


M.S. Biology at UPRM
As a master’s student in Biology at the University of Puerto Rico at Mayagüez, my graduate research focuses on Toxoplasma gondii transmission across the land-sea interface in Puerto Rico and its potential conservation implications for the endangered Caribbean manatee.

M.S. Biology Manatee Project Puerto Rico Toxoplasma gondii





5


Reproducible research and project ecosystems
I am building my work around reproducible research practices using R, RStudio, GitHub, Quarto, environmental data, molecular diagnostics, and project-specific websites that can grow into long-term research and outreach platforms.

R GitHub Quarto Project websites





6


Future direction
My long-term path is oriented toward wildlife health, veterinary science, conservation physiology, reproductive biology, One Health, and research that supports threatened species conservation, community-based science, and environmental decision-making.

Future DVM/PhD pathway Wildlife health Conservation medicine

title: About
text:
Conservation identity
I am interested in conservation science that begins with real ecological problems and moves toward useful evidence. My work is rooted in the idea that wildlife health cannot be separated from landscapes, communities, domestic animals, infrastructure, environmental change, and policy.
Puerto Rico is central to my scientific identity. Island systems make connections visible: mountains to rivers, cities to estuaries, cats to coastal waters, pathogens to wildlife, and communities to conservation action.

title: About
text:
Professional direction
I describe myself as a conservation agronomist because my academic foundation began in agriculture and animal science, but my current work extends into wildlife health, disease ecology, conservation physiology, environmental diagnostics, bioinformatics, and One Health.
My long-term goal is to build a career that integrates wildlife disease ecology, veterinary science, comparative physiology, reproductive biology, and conservation research.

title: Projects
text:
Repository strategy
The main website functions as my professional academic portal. Each major project will live in its own GitHub repository with its own Quarto website, allowing each project to grow independently while keeping a consistent visual identity.

Main portal: ajfigueroaruiz.github.io
Project websites: separate repositories published with GitHub Pages.
Future expansion: project sites, outreach platforms, educational resources, and potential shop pages can reuse this design system.

title: Research
text: My research explores how pathogens, environmental change, and human-modified landscapes influence wildlife health, ecosystem health, and One Health outcomes.

title: Research
text:
Research overview
My current work focuses on Toxoplasma gondii transmission across terrestrial, estuarine, and marine systems in Puerto Rico. I am especially interested in how oocyst contamination, runoff, stormwater pathways, coastal ecosystems, and manatee habitats may be connected through the land-sea interface.
This work is part of a broader research vision that integrates conservation physiology, wildlife medicine and disease ecology, reproductive biology, environmental diagnostics, epidemiology, bioinformatics, and community-centered conservation science.

title: Research
text:
Field-to-conservation workflow
My research connects field observations, laboratory evidence, computational analysis, mapping, outreach, and academic conservation spaces. This workflow reflects how I want my science to move: from real landscapes and samples toward reproducible evidence, conservation interpretation, and public communication.





Field sampling and ecological context



1. Field
Environmental and ecological questions begin in real landscapes, watersheds, coastal habitats, and human-modified systems.






Laboratory diagnostics and sample processing



2. Lab
Field samples become biological evidence through microscopy, molecular diagnostics, DNA extraction, qPCR workflows, and careful sample processing.






Coding and bioinformatics workflow



3. Code
R, GitHub, Quarto, data cleaning, visualization, and reproducible workflows help turn raw information into transparent analysis.






Land-sea transmission and mapping analysis



4. Mapping
Spatial thinking helps connect mountains, rivers, cities, estuaries, coastal ecosystems, and manatee habitats through the land-sea interface.






Conservation outreach with children and community education



5. Conservation outreach
Science should return to communities through education, visual communication, student training, and public-facing conservation storytelling.






Conservation in academic and community spaces



6. Conservation in academia
Academic spaces can become bridges between research, conservation partners, students, communities, and future collaborative action.





Field


→


Lab


→


Code


→


Mapping


→


Outreach


→


Academia



Scroll sideways to explore the workflow →

title: Research
text:
Research gateways


Disease ecology
Pathogen movement, environmental exposure, host-pathogen systems, and wildlife health.


Conservation physiology
Biological responses to stressors, disease, environmental change, and human-modified landscapes.


One Health
Connections among humans, animals, community animals, watersheds, coastal systems, and wildlife.


Reproducible research
R, GitHub, Quarto, mapping, bioinformatics, transparent workflows, and public documentation.

title: Research
text:
Field-to-conservation model


1. Ecological setting
Identify the landscape, species, habitat, watershed, or human-modified system where the conservation question emerges.


2. Exposure pathway
Evaluate how pathogens, stressors, or environmental contaminants may move through that system.


3. Biological evidence
Use field sampling, laboratory methods, diagnostics, and data analysis to characterize patterns.


4. Conservation interpretation
Translate results into wildlife health, One Health, education, outreach, and decision-making contexts.

title: Research
text:
Focal systems


Caribbean manatee
Marine mammal conservation, coastal habitat, environmental exposure, and health risk interpretation.


Toxoplasma gondii
Environmental persistence, land-sea movement, host-pathogen interaction, and One Health relevance.


Human diagnostic records
Retrospective epidemiological data that help build a human-health layer into Puerto Rico’s One Health picture.


Zebrafish ZF4 cells
A comparative cellular system that helps connect exposure questions to host-pathogen biology.

title: Research
text:
Methods and tools

Environmental sampling Field metadata Microscopy DNA extraction qPCR workflows R RStudio GitHub Quarto GIS Bioinformatics Scientific writing Science communication

title: Research
text:
Long-term vision
My long-term goal is to develop a research program that connects wildlife disease ecology, veterinary science, conservation physiology, reproductive biology, and One Health to understand how environmental change affects threatened species.
I am especially interested in research that is useful for conservation decision-making, transparent through reproducible workflows, and accessible to the communities connected to the ecosystems being studied.
